- 18.6.1 Provide counseling services on eye disease prevention, glaucoma management, and prevention of complications (e.g., diabetic retinopathy).
- 18.6.2 Offer support for patients with vision loss, including low vision aids and rehabilitation services.

# **19 Orthopedic Patient Management P1**

## **19.1 Orthopedic Surgeries: P1**

- 19.1.1 Record surgeon and assistants' details.
- 19.1.2 Document surgical procedure details (e.g., surgeon, procedure type such as hip replacement, knee arthroscopy, spinal fusion, and surgical notes).
- 19.1.3 Track use of implants (e.g., prosthetic joints, screws, plates, rods) and implant details (e.g., model number, serial number for tracking and post-surgical follow-up).
- 19.1.4 Document anesthesia used during surgeries (e.g., general anesthesia, regional anesthesia, nerve blocks).

## **19.2 Post-Surgical Care: P1**

- 19.2.1 Monitor post-operative recovery (pain management, wound healing, mobility, rehabilitation).
- 19.2.2 Track use of pain medications, antibiotics, and anti-thrombotic agents in the post-surgical period.
- 19.2.3 Record physiotherapy and rehabilitation plans for improving mobility, joint strength, and range of motion.

## **19.3 Orthopedic Medications: P1**

- 19.3.1 Track medications related to pain management (e.g., opioids, NSAIDs, muscle relaxants).
- 19.3.2 Document use of bone health medications (e.g., bisphosphonates, calcium supplements, vitamin D, and hormone therapy for osteoporosis).
- 19.3.3 Record prescriptions for anti-inflammatory drugs, antibiotics, and anti-coagulants for surgical patients.

## **19.4 Drug Interactions and Monitoring: P1**

- 19.4.1 Provide drug interaction alerts for pain medications, muscle relaxants, and anticoagulants.
- 19.4.2 Monitor medication adherence for chronic conditions like osteoarthritis or osteoporosis.

## **19.5 Rehabilitation Plan: P1**

- 19.5.1 Develop and document rehabilitation plans for patients recovering from fractures, joint replacement surgery, or musculoskeletal injuries.
- 19.5.2 Schedule and track physiotherapy sessions, including types of therapy (e.g., strengthening exercises, range of motion exercises, postural training).

**19.6 Progress Tracking: P1**

- 19.6.1 Monitor rehabilitation progress (e.g., improvements in mobility, pain levels, and muscle strength).
- 19.6.2 Track patient compliance with rehabilitation exercises.

**19.7 Fracture Classification: P1**

- 19.7.1 Record detailed information on fracture type (e.g., location, displacement, open or closed status).
- 19.7.2 Integrate with X-ray systems for automatic documentation of fracture imaging.

**19.8 Fracture Management: P1**

- 19.8.1 Track conservative management (e.g., casting, splinting) or surgical interventions (e.g., internal fixation, external fixation).
- 19.8.2 Monitor bone healing using radiology reports and clinical evaluation.

**19.9 Trauma Care:**

- 19.9.1 Record detailed information for patients with traumatic injuries (e.g., mechanism of injury, clinical presentation, and multi-disciplinary coordination for trauma cases).

**19.10 Patient Education: P1**

- 19.10.1 Provide educational materials on fracture care, post-surgical recovery, bone health, arthritis management, and rehabilitation exercises.
- 19.10.2 Educate patients about safety to avoid falls, proper posture, ergonomics, and preventive measures for osteoporosis and degenerative joint diseases.

**19.11 Counseling Services: P2**

- 19.11.1 Offer psychological counseling for patients recovering from major surgeries or those with chronic pain conditions (e.g., osteoarthritis, spinal disorders).
- 19.11.2 Provide guidance on lifestyle changes to improve bone health and prevent further fractures.

**20 ENT Patient Management P1****20.1 Surgical Interventions: P1**

- 20.1.1 Record surgeon and assistants' details.
- 20.1.2 Document surgical procedures such as septoplasty, sinus surgery, adenoidectomy, tonsillectomy, ear surgeries, neck dissections for head and neck cancers, and voice box surgeries.
- 20.1.3 Include surgeon's name, surgery type, surgical notes, and post-operative care instructions.

**20.2 Post-Surgical Care: P1**

- 20.2.1 Track post-surgical recovery (pain management, wound care, monitoring for complications like bleeding and infection).
- 20.2.2 Schedule and document follow-up visits to monitor recovery and assess for complications.

**20.3 ENT-Specific Medications: P1**

- 20.3.1 Track medications (e.g., antibiotics for ear or throat infections, decongestants for nasal obstructions, steroids for sinus inflammation, pain management post-surgery).
- 20.3.2 Record prescriptions for allergy medications (e.g., antihistamines, nasal sprays, topical treatments for sinus-related issues).

**20.4 Prescription Management: P1**

- 20.4.1 Ensure prescription accuracy and monitor drug interactions, especially for patients with allergies, asthma, or chronic sinusitis.

**20.5 Audiology Testing and Management: P1**

- 20.5.1 Track hearing test results (e.g., pure-tone audiometry, speech audiometry).
- 20.5.2 Document management of hearing loss (e.g., use of hearing aids, cochlear implants, surgical interventions like ossiculoplasty).
- 20.5.3 Provide counseling for hearing-impaired patients, including options for hearing devices and assistive technologies.

**20.6 Balance and Vestibular Disorders: P1**

- 20.6.1 Track balance assessments for conditions like vestibular neuritis and Meniere's disease.
- 20.6.2 Implement treatment plans for balance disorders, including vestibular rehabilitation therapy.

**20.7 Chronic Sinusitis Management: P1**

- 20.7.1 Track management of chronic sinusitis (e.g., steroid medications, sinus surgery, allergy treatments).
- 20.7.2 Monitor nasal irrigation and humidification for patients with persistent nasal congestion or sinus issues.

**20.8 Nasal Obstruction: P1**

- 20.8.1 Record treatment for nasal obstructions (e.g., deviated septum correction, polyp removal, nasal surgery).
- 20.8.2 Document patient progress post-surgery for conditions like nasal polyps or septal perforation.

**20.9 Education on Conditions: P1**

- 20.9.1 Provide patient education on conditions such as sleep apnea, sinusitis, allergic rhinitis, and hearing loss.
- 20.9.2 Educate patients about preventive care for ear infections, hearing protection, and nasal hygiene.

**20.10 Post-Surgical and Rehabilitation Counseling: P1**

- 20.10.1 Counsel patients on post-surgical care for procedures like tonsillectomy, adenoidectomy, or sinus surgery.
- 20.10.2 Offer rehabilitation support for speech therapy or hearing aid use.

**20.11 Post-Surgery Monitoring: P1**

- 20.11.1 Track post-surgery follow-ups to monitor for complications (e.g., infection, bleeding, wound healing).
- 20.11.2 Implement long-term follow-up for chronic conditions like hearing loss or sleep apnea.

## **20.12 Long-Term Management: P1**

- 20.12.1 Track chronic ENT conditions such as allergic rhinitis, chronic sinusitis, and ear conditions through regular monitoring (e.g., imaging, audiological testing, and symptom evaluation).

## **21 Neurosurgery Patient Management P1**

### **21.1 Surgical Procedure Monitoring: P1**

- 21.1.1 Record surgeon and assistants' details.
- 21.1.2 Track real-time data during surgery, including vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure), anesthesia records, and neuro-monitoring (e.g., EEG, neuromonitoring for spinal surgeries).

### **21.2 Documentation of Surgical Steps: P1**

- 21.2.1 Record key details of the procedure, such as incision type, surgical approach, and any complications encountered (e.g., bleeding, nerve damage).

### **21.3 Post-Surgical Orders: P1**

- 21.3.1 Document post-operative care orders for medication (e.g., pain relief, antibiotics), monitoring (e.g., neurological checks), and wound care.
- 21.3.2 Recovery Monitoring: P1
- 21.3.3 Track post-surgical recovery, including neurological assessments, pain management, and monitoring for complications like infection, bleeding, or seizures.
- 21.3.4 Document progress in ICU (Intensive Care Unit) or PACU (Post-Anesthesia Care Unit) for intensive monitoring.

### **21.4 Complication Management: P1**

- 21.4.1 Monitor and manage postoperative complications such as cerebral edema, infection, hemorrhage, seizures, or hydrocephalus.
- 21.4.2 Integrate with other hospital departments like pharmacy for medication management and radiology for follow-up imaging (e.g., head CT, MRI).

### **21.5 Pain Management: P1**

- 21.5.1 Document pain management protocols, including the use of analgesics, sedatives, and opioids.
- 21.5.2 Monitor pain levels using standardized pain scales (e.g., numeric rating scale, visual analog scale) to guide medication adjustments.

### **21.6 Post-Surgical Medications: P1**

- 21.6.1 Track antibiotics, anticoagulants, anti-seizure medications, and corticosteroids as prescribed post-surgery.
- 21.6.2 Ensure medication reconciliation and dose adjustments based on patient response and clinical status

### **21.7 Neurological Assessments: P1**

- 21.7.1 Continuously monitor for changes in cognitive function, motor skills, speech, and sensory function.
- 21.7.2 Record neurological exams post-surgery to assess recovery and detect signs of neurological deterioration (e.g., increased intracranial pressure).

## **21.8 Rehabilitation Plans: P1**

- 21.8.1 Develop rehabilitation plans for patients requiring physical therapy, speech therapy, or occupational therapy post-neurosurgery.
- 21.8.2 Coordinate with neurological rehabilitation teams to improve functional recovery for conditions like stroke recovery, spinal cord injuries, and post-tumor surgeries.

## **21.9 Post-Surgery Education: P2**

- 21.9.1 Provide patients and families with education on post-surgical care, including medication adherence, activity restrictions, and follow-up appointments.
- 21.9.2 Educate on symptoms of complications like increased swelling, infection, or seizures that require immediate attention.

## **21.10 Psychological Support: P2**

- 21.10.1 Offer psychological counseling for patients recovering from significant neurological surgeries, especially those dealing with functional impairments or mental health challenges.

# **22 Oncology Patient Management**

## **22.1 Surgical Treatment Management: P1**

- 22.1.1 Record surgeon and assistants' details.
- 22.1.2 Document cancer-related surgeries, including tumor resection, palliative surgeries, and reconstructive surgeries.
- 22.1.3 Ensure integration with the operating room scheduling system for oncological surgeries.
- 22.1.4 Track surgical notes and operative reports, including surgical approach, tumor removal success, and post-surgical complications.

## **22.2 Non-Surgical Treatment (Chemotherapy, Radiation, Immunotherapy): P1**

- 22.2.1 Track chemotherapy cycles (e.g., neoadjuvant, adjuvant therapy), including medication administration, infusion dates, and treatment response.
- 22.2.2 Record radiation therapy sessions, side effects, and patient response to radiation.
- 22.2.3 Integrate immunotherapy protocols and track outcomes related to immune checkpoint inhibitors, monoclonal antibodies, or CAR-T cell therapies.

## **22.3 Clinical Monitoring: P1**

- 22.3.1 Monitor vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation) for patients undergoing chemotherapy, radiotherapy, or surgeries.
- 22.3.2 Track side effects of chemotherapy (e.g., nausea, fatigue, neutropenia) and radiotherapy (e.g., skin burns, fatigue, radiation-induced sickness).

## **22.4 Oncology-Specific Monitoring: P1**

- 22.4.1 Track tumor markers such as CEA, AFP, CA-125, PSA, and HER2 to assess tumor burden and treatment response.
- 22.4.2 Record imaging results (CT, MRI, PET scan) to monitor for tumor shrinkage, recurrence, or metastasis.

## **22.5 Follow-Up Visits: P1**

- 22.5.1 Schedule and track follow-up appointments for cancer patients after treatment (e.g., post-chemotherapy, post-radiation, post-surgery).
- 22.5.2 Monitor for recurrence or metastasis and manage late effects of treatment, such as secondary cancers or cardiac complications from chemotherapy.

## **22.6 Symptom Management: P1**

- 22.6.1 Track and manage palliative care needs for pain control, nausea, vomiting, anorexia, fatigue, and other symptoms.
- 22.6.2 Integrate pain management protocols and palliative care teams to provide comfort care and address quality of life concerns.

## **22.7 Psychosocial Support: P2**

- 22.7.1 Provide access to psychosocial counseling for depression, anxiety, fear of recurrence, and end-of-life care.
- 22.7.2 Track patient and family counseling sessions, offering emotional support and education about the cancer journey.

## **22.8 Nutritional Support: P1**

- 22.8.1 Manage nutritional interventions for cancer patients, especially those undergoing chemotherapy or radiation therapy facing weight loss, malnutrition, and digestive issues.

## **22.9 Chemotherapy and Supportive Medications: P1**

- 22.9.1 Track chemotherapy regimens, including pre-medications (e.g., antiemetic, growth factors), chemotherapy drugs, and post-treatment medications.
- 22.9.2 Manage medication orders for pain relief, antibiotics (for infection prevention), and growth factors to support immune function during chemotherapy.

## **22.10 Drug Interaction Management: P1**

- 22.10.1 Ensure drug interaction checking for chemotherapy agents, targeted therapies, immunotherapy, and supportive medications (e.g., anticoagulants, antidepressants).

## **22.11 Laboratory Integration: P1**

- 22.11.1 Integrate with laboratory systems to track cancer-related tests, including tumor markers, complete blood count (CBC), liver and kidney function tests, and genetic testing.
- 22.11.2 Document test results for biopsy, pathology reports, and genomic sequencing for precision medicine.

## **22.12 Imaging Integration: P1**

- 22.12.1 Integrate with radiology to capture imaging reports for tumor staging and monitoring (e.g., PET scans, MRI, CT scans).

# **23 PATIENTS ALERTS AND MONITORING P1**

## **23.1 Vital Signs Monitoring: P1**

- 23.1.1 Real-time collection and display of vital signs data, including:

- 23.1.1.1 Heart rate (HR)
- 23.1.1.2 Blood pressure (BP)
- 23.1.1.3 Respiratory rate (RR)
- 23.1.1.4 Oxygen saturation (SpO<sub>2</sub>)
- 23.1.1.5 Body temperature
- 23.1.1.6 Electrocardiogram (ECG) readings (if applicable)

## **23.2 Continuous Vital Sign Monitoring: P1**

- 23.2.1 Integration with patient monitoring devices (e.g., heart rate monitors, blood pressure cuffs, oxygen sensors) for continuous tracking of vital signs.
- 23.2.2 ICU and Critical Care Monitoring: For patients in intensive care, monitors track parameters like intracranial pressure, central venous pressure, and arterial blood gases.

## **23.3 Integration with Wearable Devices: P2**

- 23.3.1 Integration with wearable devices (e.g., continuous glucose monitors, ECG monitors) to transmit real-time data to the system.
- 23.3.2 Devices send alerts if data deviates from pre-set thresholds, enabling prompt intervention.

## **23.4 Data Visualization: P2**

- 23.4.1 Real-time visualization of patient data on dashboards, displaying trends and abnormalities.
- 23.4.2 Time-series graphs for vital signs and monitored parameters to spot trends (e.g., rising BP, declining SpO<sub>2</sub>).

## **23.5 Device Integration: P2**

- 23.5.1 System integrates with various monitoring devices (e.g., heart rate monitors, ECG machines, pulse oximeters, temperature probes) for automatic data capture and updates to patient records.

## **23.6 Continuous Tracking: P2**

- 23.6.1 Continuous monitoring of patient vitals, with alerts for abnormal readings (e.g., rising blood pressure or dropping oxygen saturation).

## **23.7 Threshold-Based Alerts: P2**

- 23.7.1 Predefined thresholds for vital signs trigger automated alerts when measurements exceed or fall below set levels.
- 23.7.2 Example: Alert if BP exceeds 180/120 mmHg or SpO<sub>2</sub> drops below 90%.

## **23.8 Critical Alerts: P1**

- 23.8.1 Life-threatening situations trigger critical alerts for immediate intervention (e.g., cardiac arrest, severe arrhythmias, and respiratory failure).

## **23.9 Multi-Channel Alerts: P2**

- 23.9.1 Alerts sent through multiple channels (email, SMS, in-app notifications) to ensure healthcare providers are informed.
- 23.9.2 Mobile app integration for alerts sent to mobile devices, allowing for response from any location.

**23.10 Escalation of Alerts: P2**

23.10.1 Unaddressed alerts can escalate to other healthcare providers (e.g., nurse to physician or specialist) to ensure timely action.

**23.11 Configurable Alert Sensitivity: P2**

23.11.1 Allows healthcare providers to set alert sensitivity based on hospital policies, patient types, or department needs (e.g., ICU, ER).

23.11.2 Non-critical alerts can be less intrusive to avoid alarm fatigue.

**23.12 Alert Categories: P2**

23.12.1 Alerts categorized by urgency:

23.12.1.1 Critical Alerts: Severe deviations requiring immediate intervention (e.g., cardiac arrest, respiratory failure).

23.12.1.2 High Alerts: Moderate changes requiring prompt attention (e.g., abnormal oxygen saturation, elevated temperature).

23.12.1.3 Low Alerts: Minor deviations for routine care (e.g., mild fever).

**23.13 Multiple Alert Mechanisms: P2**

23.13.1 Visual alerts on-screen, audible alerts, SMS/email notifications, and mobile app alerts.

23.13.2 Alerts routed to appropriate staff, including physicians, nurses, and specialists.

**23.14 Early Warning Scores (EWS): P2**

23.14.1 Use of scoring systems (e.g., MEWS, APACHE, GCS) to assess patient risk based on vital signs and clinical data.

**23.15 Risk Stratification: P2**

23.15.1 System calculates the risk of complications or deterioration using vital signs, medical history, and current health conditions.

**23.16 Predictive Analytics: P2**

23.16.1 Machine learning algorithms analyze trends in patient data to predict future events, such as the risk of sepsis, cardiac arrest, or respiratory failure.

**23.17 ICU/CCU Monitoring: P1**

23.17.1 Continuous real-time monitoring of ICU/CCU patients' vital signs and organ functions.

23.17.2 Centralized monitoring stations display multiple patient vital signs for immediate action.

**23.18 Critical Event Detection: P2**

23.18.1 System automatically identifies critical events (e.g., cardiac arrhythmias, respiratory distress) and triggers alerts for immediate intervention.

**23.19 Early Deterioration Detection: P2**

23.19.1 Predictive analytics detect early signs of deterioration (e.g., slight blood pressure drop or increase in heart rate) and predict adverse events before they occur.

**23.20 Scoring Systems Integration: P2**

23.20.1 Integration of early warning scores (e.g., NEWS2, MEWS) to assess patient risk and prioritize interventions.

### **23.21 Pattern Recognition: P2**

23.21.1Machine learning analyzes trends in vital signs and patient history to predict potential issues (e.g., acute respiratory distress or shock).

### **23.22 Proactive Interventions: P2**

23.22.1Predictive alerts enable proactive interventions to prevent complications or death (e.g., hypotension after surgery).

### **23.23 Immediate Alerts for Code Blue or Emergency: P1**

23.23.1Critical events (e.g., cardiac arrest) trigger immediate alerts to resuscitation teams via mobile devices, pagers, or alarms.

### **23.24 Automated Activation of Protocols: P2**

23.24.1Upon detection of critical events, the system can automatically trigger emergency protocols (e.g., CPR, defibrillation).

### **23.25 Real-Time Event Documentation: P1**

23.25.1System logs every action during a critical event, including interventions, medication, and time stamps, for compliance and analysis.

### **23.26 Alarm Fatigue Management: P2**

23.26.1Reduces alarm fatigue by prioritizing critical alerts and adjusting thresholds based on patient condition.

### **23.27 Infusion Pumps and Ventilators Integration: P2**

23.27.1Monitors infusion rates, ventilation parameters (e.g., oxygen delivery, pressure settings), and patient responses in real-time.

### **23.28 Electronic Medication Administration Records (eMAR): P2**

23.28.1Integration with eMAR tracks medication administration and alerts for mismatches or interactions with other treatments.

### **23.29 Multiple Alert Recipients: P2**

23.29.1Alerts can be routed to different recipients based on urgency (e.g., nurse for initial alert, physician for worsening condition).

### **23.30 Trend Visualization: P2**

23.30.1Visual representation of historical vital sign trends to assess patient condition over time.

### **23.31 Comparative Analysis: P2**

23.31.1Compare current vital signs with baseline values for better clinical decision-making.

### **23.32 Customizable Alert Thresholds: P2**

23.32.1Provides can customize alert thresholds for different clinical guidelines, patient conditions, or specialties.

### **23.33 Alert Sound and Frequency Customization: P2**

23.33.1Users can adjust alert sounds, frequency, and repetition to distinguish urgent alerts from non-emergency notifications.

### **23.34 Alert Summary and Reporting: P2**

23.34.1Generate reports summarizing alerts, response times, and resolutions to monitor performance and improve efficiency.

### **23.35 Customized Patient Alerts: P2**

23.35.1 Specific alert protocols based on the patient's medical condition, treatment regimen, or surgery (e.g., blood pressure alerts for post-surgical patients).

### **23.36 Patient Safety Alerts: P2**

23.36.1 Generate alerts for fall risk, pressure ulcers, or infection risks (e.g., catheter-associated UTIs, ventilator-associated pneumonia).

### **23.37 Alert Acknowledgement: P2**

23.37.1 Healthcare providers must acknowledge alerts, ensuring they are aware and acted upon.

### **23.38 Team Collaboration: P2**

23.38.1 Facilitates communication among healthcare providers via integrated messaging to coordinate actions in response to critical alerts.

### **23.39 Integration with PACS and EHR: P1**

23.39.1 Links alerts related to imaging (e.g., abnormal CT scans) directly to the patient's medical record.

### **23.40 Audit Trails: P2**

23.40.1 Logs every alert and response for audit purposes, quality improvement, and regulatory compliance.

## **24 PATIENTS TRANSFER AND DISCHARGE P1**

### **24.1 Patient Transfer**

24.1.1 Transfer Request Initiation: P1

24.1.1.1 Authorized staff (doctors, nurses) create transfer requests within the HMIS.

24.1.1.2 Details include patient condition, transfer reason, destination, required care level, and special instructions.

24.1.2 Authorization Workflow: P1

24.1.2.1 Transfer request approved by necessary healthcare professionals (e.g., attending physician, ICU doctor).

24.1.2.2 Approval includes special instructions (e.g., medication administration, oxygen support).

24.1.3 Transfer Coordination Dashboard: P1

24.1.3.1 Dashboard to track pending, in-progress, and completed transfers.

24.1.3.2 Real-time updates ensure timely transfers and no overlooked requests.

24.1.4 Automated Notifications and Alerts: P1

24.1.4.1 Alerts sent to relevant staff (e.g., receiving ward, transport team).

24.1.4.2 Notifications ensure availability of personnel and equipment.

24.1.5 Interdepartmental Communication: P1

24.1.5.1 Easy communication between departments for seamless transfer preparation.

24.1.5.2 Ensures the receiving department has necessary patient details.

24.1.6 Transport Staff Assignment: P1

- 24.1.6.1 Assigns transport staff based on patient condition, destination, and staff availability.
- 24.1.7 Transport Equipment Management: P1
  - 24.1.7.1 Tracks availability and readiness of necessary transport equipment (e.g., stretcher, oxygen tanks).
- 24.1.8 Transfer Safety Checklist: P1
  - 24.1.8.1 Ensures safety protocols are followed, such as securing the patient and verifying monitoring equipment.
- 24.1.9 Real-Time Status Tracking: P1
  - 24.1.9.1 Provides updates on transfer status, location, and arrival.
- 24.1.10 Transfer Logs: P1
  - 24.1.10.1 Logs all transfers with details such as time, reason, and involved staff.
- 24.1.11 Emergency Transfer Support: P1
  - 24.1.11.1 Allows immediate updates for emergency transfers, ensuring receiving departments are prepared.
- 24.1.12 Electronic Transfer Record: P1
  - 24.1.12.1 Generates electronic transfer records for easy access in the patient's medical record.
- 24.1.13 Transfer Notes: P1
  - 24.1.13.1 Allows documentation of specific transfer instructions and notes to assist the receiving team.
- 24.1.14 Patient Care Continuity: P1
  - 24.1.14.1 Integration with Electronic Health Records (EHR) ensures seamless patient information transfer.
- 24.1.15 Receiving Ward Notifications: P1
  - 24.1.15.1 Notifications sent to receiving ward to confirm patient arrival and initiate immediate care.
- 24.1.16 Patient Care Handover: P1
  - 24.1.16.1 Generates a handover report summarizing the patient's condition, treatments, and needs.
- 24.1.17 Post-Transfer Monitoring: P1
  - 24.1.17.1 Tracks patient condition after transfer to ensure appropriate monitoring.

## **24.2 Patient Discharge**

- 24.2.1 Early Discharge Planning: P1
  - 24.2.1.1 Discharge planning begins early to assess readiness, arrange follow-up care, and coordinate with other providers.
- 24.2.2 Multidisciplinary Involvement: P1
  - 24.2.2.1 Involves physicians, nurses, social workers, and specialists in discharge planning.
- 24.2.3 Discharge Criteria: P1

- 24.2.3.1 Tracks clinical criteria like recovery status and stabilization for discharge readiness.
- 24.2.4 Care Needs Assessment: P1
  - 24.2.4.1 Assesses ongoing care needs (e.g., home care, rehabilitation) before discharge.
- 24.2.5 Patient and Family Education: P1
  - 24.2.5.1 Educates the patient and family about post-discharge care (e.g., wound care, medication).
- 24.2.6 Discharge Request Creation: P1
  - 24.2.6.1 Authorized professionals initiate discharge requests, recording reasons, condition, and follow-up needs.
- 24.2.7 Final Review by Physician: P1
  - 24.2.7.1 Attending physician reviews discharge status and final instructions.
- 24.2.8 Approval Workflow: P1
  - 24.2.8.1 Ensures all relevant staff approve the discharge plan before finalizing.
- 24.2.9 Discharge Notification: P1
  - 24.2.9.1 Generates notification for departments (billing, pharmacy) to begin post-discharge processes.
- 24.2.10 Discharge Authorization: P1
  - 24.2.10.1 Physician confirms patient readiness and post-discharge monitoring.
- 24.2.11 Discharge Summary Generation: P1
  - 24.2.11.1 Generates a summary including diagnosis, treatments, surgeries, medications, and follow-up care.
- 24.2.12 Electronic Discharge Summary: P1
  - 24.2.12.1 Includes patient details, diagnosis, treatments, vital signs, and treatment plan.
- 24.2.13 Standardized Format: P1
  - 24.2.13.1 Ensures consistency across healthcare providers.
- 24.2.14 Medication Reconciliation: P1
  - 24.2.14.1 Compares pre-admission and post-discharge medications to prevent errors.
- 24.2.15 Prescription Generation: P1
  - 24.2.15.1 Generates e-prescriptions for post-discharge medications.
- 24.2.16 Patient Education on Medications: P1
  - 24.2.16.1 Provides materials on medication usage and adherence.
- 24.2.17 Patient Record Updates: P1
  - 24.2.17.1 Updates the EHR with discharge details, including care plans and follow-up instructions.
- 24.2.18 Discharge Certificate: P1
  - 24.2.18.1 Generates a certificate documenting the completion of hospitalization.
- 24.2.19 Audit Trails: P1

- 24.2.19.1 Logs all discharge actions for quality assurance and compliance.
- 24.2.20 Medical Record Update: P1
  - 24.2.20.1 Updates the EHR with hospitalization and discharge data.
- 24.2.21 Discharge Instructions: P1
  - 24.2.21.1 Generates instructions for medications, wound care, dietary restrictions, and follow-up appointments.
- 24.2.22 Final Billing: P1
  - 24.2.22.1 Generates final bill based on room charges, procedures, medications, and services.
- 24.2.23 Insurance Verification: P2
  - 24.2.23.1 Verifies final insurance claims for reimbursement.
- 24.2.24 Billing and Charges: P1
  - 24.2.24.1 Automatically generates the final bill, including itemized charges.
- 24.2.25 Payment Tracking: P1
  - 24.2.25.1 Tracks payments, co-pays, or balances to ensure dues are cleared.
- 24.2.26 Financial Counseling: P1
  - 24.2.26.1 Assists patients with understanding their bill and payment options.
- 24.2.27 Payment Processing: P2
  - 24.2.27.1 Processes payments, either from the patient or insurance.
- 24.2.28 Discharge Medications: P1
  - 24.2.28.1 Generates discharge prescriptions and ensures availability at the pharmacy.
- 24.2.29 Medication Education: P1
  - 24.2.29.1 Provides educational materials on prescribed medications.
- 24.2.30 Follow-Up Appointments: P1
  - 24.2.30.1 Ensures follow-up appointments are scheduled before discharge.
- 24.2.31 Referral to Specialists: P2
  - 24.2.31.1 Facilitates specialist referrals and tracks appointment scheduling.
- 24.2.32 Remote Monitoring: P2
  - 24.2.32.1 May facilitate remote recovery monitoring (e.g., telemedicine).
- 24.2.33 Patient and Family Communication: P2
  - 24.2.33.1 Provides tools for post-discharge communication between patients and healthcare providers.
- 24.2.34 Follow-Up Appointment Scheduling: P1
  - 24.2.34.1 Schedules follow-up visits based on the patient's condition.
- 24.2.35 Referral Management: P1
  - 24.2.35.1 Manages referrals to specialists, recording and communicating necessary details.
- 24.2.36 Comprehensive Discharge Instructions: P1
  - 24.2.36.1 Provides detailed discharge instructions, including wound care, dietary restrictions, and warning signs.
- 24.2.37 Discharge Education Materials: P2
  - 24.2.37.1 Supplies educational resources on recovery and care.
- 24.2.38 Patient Understanding Confirmation: P2

- 24.2.38.1 Confirms patient understanding of discharge instructions.
- 24.2.39 Patient Feedback and Satisfaction: P1
  - 24.2.39.1 Collects feedback to improve hospital services.
- 24.2.40 Readmission Alerts: P2
  - 24.2.40.1 Tracks patients at risk for readmission and prompts follow-up actions.
- 24.2.41 Clinical Follow-Up: P2
  - 24.2.41.1 Schedules or sends reminders for clinical follow-up visits.
- 24.2.42 Follow-Up Notifications: P2
  - 24.2.42.1 Sends reminders for appointments or tests after discharge.
- 24.2.43 Post-Discharge Surveys: P2
  - 24.2.43.1 Collects patient feedback on the discharge process.
- 24.2.44 Outcome Monitoring: P2
  - 24.2.44.1 Monitors patient outcomes post-discharge to detect complications.

25.5.3 Water Quality Monitoring: P1

25.6.1 Security Systems: P1

25.6.1.2 Integrate emergency security protocols such as lockdown procedures, patient abduction prevention, and staff safety.

25.6.2 Access Control and Role-Based Permissions: P1

25.6.2.1 Ensure restricted access to sensitive areas.

25.6.2.2 Monitor staff access based on role-based permissions to restrict access to appropriate areas (e.g., patient care areas for doctors, nurses, and authorized staff).

## **26 IPD STAFF SCHEDULING AND MANAGEMENT P1:**

### **26.1 Staff Availability and Shift Management**

### 30.1 Reporting

30.1.1.4 Tools for reviewing and analyzing incidents.

30.1.2.1 Secure messaging system for communication between doctors, nurses, and other medical staff.

30.1.2.2 Real-time updates about the patient's condition, test results, or required actions.

30.1.3 Handoff Notes: P1

30.1.3.1 Summarized patient information for shift changes or transfers.

30.1.4 Automated Alerts and Notifications: P2

- 30.1.4.1 Immediate alerts for critical conditions, urgent tests, or changes in patient status.
- 30.1.4.2 Notification system for incoming ambulances, critical cases, and triage updates.
- 30.1.5 Team Coordination: P1
  - 30.1.5.1 Task assignment to medical teams based on priorities (e.g., triage, treatment, diagnostic procedures).
  - 30.1.5.2 Group messaging features for cross-functional collaboration between IPD staff.
- 30.1.6 Referral System: P1
  - 30.1.6.1 Streamlined process for referring patients to other departments or specialists.
- 30.1.7 Patient Messaging: P2
  - 30.1.7.1 Update patients on their condition or send reminders (e.g., test results, follow-up care instructions).

### **31.1 Integration System**

- 31.1.2 Laboratory and Imaging Systems: P1
  - 31.1.2.1 Direct ordering and viewing of lab tests and imaging results.
- 31.1.3 Pharmacy: P1
  - 31.1.3.1 Integration for medication orders and inventory checks.
- 31.1.4 Billing System: P1
  - 31.1.4.1 Automatic billing updates based on procedures, consultations, and tests.
- 31.1.5 Hospital Management System (HMS): P1
  - 31.1.5.1 Integration with admission, discharge, and transfer (ADT) systems to track patient location and bed management.

## **32 MORTALITY DATA AND REVIEW**

### **32.1 Mortality Data Requirements**

- 32.1.1 Patient Demographics: P1
  - 32.1.1.1 Patient ID (Unique identifier)
  - 32.1.1.2 Age
  - 32.1.1.3 Sex
  - 32.1.1.4 CNIC #
  - 32.1.1.5 Date of admission
  - 32.1.1.6 Date of death
  - 32.1.1.7 Admission diagnosis/Primary reason for ICU admission
- 32.1.2 Clinical Information: P1

- 32.1.2.1 Admission severity (e.g., APACHE score, SAPS, etc.)
- 32.1.2.2 Underlying comorbidities (e.g., heart disease, diabetes, cancer, etc.)
- 32.1.2.3 ICU interventions (e.g., mechanical ventilation, renal replacement therapy, etc.)
- 32.1.2.4 Organ failure details (e.g., cardiovascular, respiratory, renal failure)
- 32.1.2.5 Final diagnosis or cause of death (as determined by the attending physician)
- 32.1.2.6 Procedures performed (e.g., surgeries, invasive monitoring)
- 32.1.3 Mortality Type: P1
  - 32.1.3.1 In-hospital mortality (death during the stay)
  - 32.1.3.2 Out-of-hospital mortality (if applicable)
  - 32.1.3.3 Timing of death (during stay vs. after discharge but within the same hospital stay)
- 32.1.4 Cause of Death Classification: P1
  - 32.1.4.1 Classification of cause of death (primary illness, complications during treatment, or other factors like infection, sepsis, organ failure, etc.)

## **32.2 Mortality Review Process**

- 32.2.1 Structured Mortality Review: P1
  - 32.2.1.1 Mortality Review Committee: Multidisciplinary team (e.g., intensivists, nurses, pharmacists, etc.) conducting the review
  - 32.2.1.2 Review Meetings: Regular meetings to review each death with structured evaluations of clinical course, decisions, and adherence to best practices
  - 32.2.1.3 Case Review: Evaluation of whether death was anticipated, preventable factors, adherence to protocols, and delays in diagnosis/treatment
  - 32.2.1.4 Root Cause Analysis: If needed, deeper analysis to identify systemic issues or clinical errors
- 32.2.2 Data Integration for Review: P1
  - 32.2.2.1 Integration of mortality data (structured data, patient demographics, lab results, and unstructured data like clinician notes)
  - 32.2.2.2 Utilization of data from electronic health records (EHR) and IPD monitoring systems in reviews
- 32.2.3 Feedback Loop: P1
  - 32.2.3.1 Actionable feedback for clinical teams based on mortality review findings (e.g., changes to treatment protocols, updates to patient monitoring, or improvement in staff training)
  - 32.2.3.2 Implementation of improvements and tracking of outcomes
- 32.2.4 Identification of Preventable Mortality: P1
  - 32.2.4.1 Identification of preventable deaths and clinical practice improvements
  - 32.2.4.2 Improvements in early detection systems, interventions, or communication strategies

## **32.3 HMIS Integration of Mortality Data & Review**

- 32.3.1 Data Entry & Standardization: P1

- 32.3.1.1 Consistent data collection using predefined templates and classification systems (e.g., causes of death, APACHE scores)
- 32.3.1.2 Automated Reporting: P2
- 32.3.1.3 Automation of mortality rate reports, causes of death, and trends over time
- 32.3.2 Analytics & Dashboards: P2
  - 32.3.2.1 Tools to analyze mortality trends, compare with benchmarks, and identify areas for improvement
- 32.3.3 Documentation & Case History: P1
  - 32.3.3.1 Easy access to patient data for the review team during mortality review sessions

### **33 DASHBOARD – REPORTING & ANALYTICS P1**

#### **33.1 Electronic Medical Records (EMR) Creation and Management**

- 33.1.1 Patient Medical Records (PMR): P1
  - 33.1.1.1 Facilitates the creation of electronic patient medical records.
  - 33.1.1.2 Captures personal demographics, medical history, allergies, diagnoses, treatment plans, surgeries, and test results.
- 33.1.2 Patient Profile: P1
  - 33.1.2.1 Compiles a comprehensive patient profile, including personal information, medical history, and family history.
  - 33.1.2.2 Allows clinicians to view all relevant patient details in a single interface.
- 33.1.3 Documenting Medical Conditions: P1
  - 33.1.3.1 Documents and regularly updates chronic conditions (e.g., diabetes, hypertension), acute conditions (e.g., infections, trauma), and historical diagnoses.
- 33.1.4 Patient Demographics: P1
  - 33.1.4.1 Reports on patient characteristics, such as:
    - 33.1.4.1.1 Age, Gender, and Ethnicity: Insights into the hospital population's composition.
    - 33.1.4.1.2 Geographical Trends: Analyzes the location of patients (city, region, country) and identifies local health trends.
- 33.1.5 Patient Admission and Discharge Trends: P1
  - 33.1.5.1 Admission Patterns: Tracks changes in the volume and types of patient admissions (e.g., seasonal variations, disease outbreaks).
  - 33.1.5.2 Length of Stay (LOS): Analyzes trends in the average length of stay (ALOS) for various medical conditions or departments.
- 33.1.6 Health Condition Trends: P2
  - 33.1.6.1 Identifies emerging health conditions or disease outbreaks by tracking the prevalence of specific diagnoses over time.
- 33.1.7 Admission Records: P1

33.1.7.1 Captures admission-related details, including:

33.1.7.1.1 Reason for admission

33.1.7.1.2 Attending physician

33.1.7.1.3 Admission date

33.1.7.1.4 Room/bed allocation

33.1.7.2 Automatically links these records to the patient's medical record.

33.1.8 Visit History: P1

33.1.8.1 Records every patient visit to the hospital (for outpatient or inpatient care) with relevant clinical information, diagnostics, and treatments.

33.1.9 Tracking Multiple Admissions: P1

33.1.9.1 Tracks a patient's multiple hospital admissions, ensuring all historical and current records are linked and easily accessible.

33.1.10 Admission History: P1

33.1.10.1 Maintains a complete history of all patient admissions, helping with long-term tracking of conditions, hospital stays, and billing.

### **33.2 Performance Data, Reporting and Analytics**

33.2.1 Doctor Performance Analytics: P1

33.2.1.1 Tracks metrics such as:

33.2.1.1.1 Patient outcomes

33.2.1.1.2 Number of consultations

33.2.1.1.3 Surgeries performed

33.2.1.1.4 Compliance with clinical guidelines

33.2.1.2 Includes peer reviews and quality assurance metrics

33.2.1.3 Aggregates data from:

33.2.1.3.1 Consultation Reports

33.2.1.3.2 Surgical Reports

33.2.1.3.3 Patient Outcomes

33.2.1.4 Enables long-term trend analysis for diseases and treatments

33.2.2 Nurse Performance and Evaluation

- 33.2.2.1 Tracks individual nurse performance based on:
  - 33.2.2.1.1 Task completion
  - 33.2.2.1.2 Patient outcomes
  - 33.2.2.1.3 Feedback from other staff members
- 33.2.2.2 Evaluates nurse performance using predefined criteria such as:
  - 33.2.2.2.1 Patient satisfaction
  - 33.2.2.2.2 Quality of care
  - 33.2.2.2.3 Timely task execution
- 33.2.2.3 Integrates with HR systems for efficiency and performance assessments.
- 33.2.2.4 Training and Development: P1
  - 33.2.2.4.1 Links to training modules for ongoing education.
  - 33.2.2.4.2 Tracks certifications and skill development.
  - 33.2.2.4.3 Ensures nurses stay updated with current best practices and standards.
- 33.2.2.5 Workload Monitoring: P2
  - 33.2.2.5.1 Monitors nurse workload in real time.
  - 33.2.2.5.2 Ensures equitable task distribution to avoid staff burnout.
  - 33.2.2.5.3 Promotes balanced staffing and improves nurse satisfaction.
- 33.2.2.6 Workload Distribution Reports: P2
  - 33.2.2.6.1 Generates reports on how tasks and patient loads are shared among staff.
  - 33.2.2.6.2 Aids in optimizing staffing and resource allocation.
- 33.2.3 Clinical Outcome Reporting: P2
  - 33.2.3.1 Tracks recovery rates, complications, and readmissions
  - 33.2.3.2 Evaluates treatment effectiveness
- 33.2.4 Performance Metrics: P1
  - 33.2.4.1 Reports on:
    - 33.2.4.1.1 IPD operations (wait times, time to assessment, patient volume)
    - 33.2.4.1.2 Staff performance and productivity
    - 33.2.4.1.3 Diagnostic test turnaround times
    - 33.2.4.1.4 IPD throughput per shift
  - 33.2.4.2 Visualizations for key metrics
- 33.2.5 Patient Queue & Tracking: P1
  - 33.2.5.1 Active patient list with names, assessment levels, status
  - 33.2.5.2 Real-time tracking in IPD (e.g., waiting room to procedure room)
  - 33.2.5.3 Search functionality
- 33.2.6 Dashboards: P2
  - 33.2.6.1 Physician and nurse dashboards for real-time patient and task updates
  - 33.2.6.2 Role-based:
    - 33.2.6.2.1 Administrative: financials, resource allocation
    - 33.2.6.2.2 Clinical: treatment progress, outcomes
  - 33.2.6.3 Customizable, interactive (pie charts, bar graphs, heat maps)
- 33.2.7 Clinical Outcomes & Compliance: P2

- 33.2.7.1 Analyzes outcomes to improve care protocols
  - 33.2.7.2 Generates reports for regulatory audits and compliance
- 33.2.8 Service Data: P1
  - 33.2.8.1 Patients visited and admitted
  - 33.2.8.2 Deaths and deaths reviewed by MRC
- 33.2.9 Bed Occupancy & Room Management: P1
  - 33.2.9.1 Real-time tracking of:
    - 33.2.9.1.1 Bed availability, occupancy, allocation
    - 33.2.9.1.2 Room utilization (ICU, maternity, etc.)
    - 33.2.9.1.3 Bed turnover (cleaning, sterilization alerts)
  - 33.2.9.2 Manages:
    - 33.2.9.2.1 Patient movement and transfers
    - 33.2.9.2.2 Bed reservation for future admissions
    - 33.2.9.2.3 Waitlists for high-demand rooms
    - 33.2.9.2.4 Room availability alerts
- 33.2.10 Occupancy Reports P1
  - 33.2.10.1 Bed utilization by department
  - 33.2.10.2 Identifies high-demand periods
  - 33.2.10.3 Optimizes resource usage
- 33.2.11 Clinical Performance Reports P1
  - 33.2.11.1 Treatment outcomes (recovery, readmission, complications)
  - 33.2.11.2 Adherence to protocols (e.g., medication, infection control)
  - 33.2.11.3 Patient progress and clinical indicators (e.g., mortality rates)
  - 33.2.11.4 Diagnosis & Treatment Analytics P1
- 33.2.12 Tracks: P2
  - 33.2.12.1 Disease prevalence
  - 33.2.12.2 Common treatments
  - 33.2.12.3 Outcomes correlated with treatments
- 33.2.13 Discharge Trends: P1
  - 33.2.13.1 Types: planned vs unplanned, condition at discharge
  - 33.2.13.2 Discharge delays and reasons (e.g., test results, insurance)
- 33.2.14 Mortality Reports: P1
  - 33.2.14.1 IPD mortality rates (standardized)
  - 33.2.14.2 Avoidable mortality
  - 33.2.14.3 Cause-specific mortality (e.g., sepsis, cardiac)
- 33.2.15 Financial Reports & Cost Analysis: P2

- 33.2.15.1 Revenue from services
  - 33.2.15.1.1 Expense breakdown (labor, supplies, utilities)
- 33.2.15.2 Billing status (outstanding, insurance claims)
- 33.2.15.3 Insurance reimbursement tracking
- 33.2.15.4 Detailed patient bills and payment trends
- 33.2.15.5 Cost per patient/service
  - 33.2.15.5.1 Identifies opportunities for cost savings
- 33.2.16 Resource Utilization Reports: P2
  - 33.2.16.1 Equipment and inventory usage
  - 33.2.16.2 Real-time stock levels and reorder schedules
- 33.2.17 Hospital Service Efficiency: P2
  - 33.2.17.1 Turnaround and procedure times
  - 33.2.17.2 Service preparation efficiency (surgery, diagnostics)
- 33.2.18 Quality Control Reports: P2
  - 33.2.18.1 Patient satisfaction and feedback
  - 33.2.18.2 Incident and infection control reporting
- 33.2.19 Predictive Modeling & Trend Analysis: P2
  - 33.2.19.1 Readmission risk prediction
  - 33.2.19.2 Resource demand forecasting
  - 33.2.19.3 Disease and financial trend forecasting
- 33.2.20 Workforce Performance & HR Analytics: P1
  - 33.2.20.1 Performance Reviews & Competency Tracking
    - 33.2.20.1.1 Doctor evaluations
    - 33.2.20.1.2 Skills and training history
    - 33.2.20.1.3 Professional development (CPD)
    - 33.2.20.1.4 Identifies competency gaps
  - 33.2.20.2 Staff Utilization & Efficiency
  - 33.2.20.3 Analyze workforce usage and shift effectiveness
  - 33.2.20.4 Monitors retention and turnover metrics
- 33.2.21 Pharmacy & Medication Analytics: P2
  - 33.2.21.1 Medication Usage Report
    - 33.2.21.1.1 Drug usage trends, volume, and costs
    - 33.2.21.1.2 Real-time stock consumption reports
  - 33.2.21.2 Adverse Event Reporting
    - 33.2.21.2.1 ADE monitoring
    - 33.2.21.2.2 Medication compliance and administration audits
  - 33.2.21.3 Regulatory Compliance
    - 33.2.21.3.1 Drug inventory and controlled substance handling audits
- 33.2.22 Departmental Outcome Reporting: P1

### 33.2.22.1 Surgical Outcomes

33.2.22.1.1 Post-op complications, length of stay, recovery rates

33.2.22.1.2 Surgeon performance, surgical volume

### 33.2.22.2 Obstetric & Gynecological

33.2.22.2.1 Delivery complications, cesarean rates, maternal mortality

33.2.22.2.2 Outcomes of obstetric treatments

### 33.2.22.3 Ophthalmology

33.2.22.3.1 Surgical success, complication rates (e.g., IOP after cataract surgery)

33.2.22.3.2 Trends in ophthalmic conditions

### 33.2.22.4 Orthopedics

33.2.22.4.1 Trends in fracture types and injury patterns

33.2.22.4.2 Rehab outcomes and pain management efficacy

### 33.2.22.5 ENT

33.2.22.5.1 Hearing loss, infection rates post-surgery

33.2.22.5.2 Trends in ENT conditions and treatment outcomes

### 33.2.22.6 Neurosurgery

33.2.22.6.1 Outcomes for brain, spine, and vascular surgeries

33.2.22.6.2 Complications and recovery metrics

### 33.2.22.7 Cancer Care

33.2.22.7.1 Incidence and survival rates for various cancer types

33.2.22.7.2 Subtype and treatment-specific outcome monitoring

33.2.22.7.3 National registry compliance

### 33.2.23.1 Transfer Reports

33.2.23.1.1 Transfer volume, timing, department patterns

33.2.23.1.2 Optimizes patient flow

## 33.3 Resource and Logistics Management

### 33.3.1 Bed Management: PI

33.3.1.1 Real-time tracking of IPD bed availability

33.3.1.2 Visual indicators for bed occupancy, cleaning status, and readiness

### 33.3.2 Staff Scheduling: PI

33.3.2.1 Intelligent scheduling based on patient load and department needs

33.3.2.2 Automation support for shift planning and conflict resolution

### 33.3.3 Equipment Tracking: PI

- 33.3.3.1 Real-time monitoring of critical equipment (e.g., ventilators, defibrillators)
  - 33.3.3.2 Dropdowns/fields to track:
    - 33.3.3.2.1 Equipment location
    - 33.3.3.2.2 Operational status
    - 33.3.3.2.3 Maintenance schedule
- 33.3.4 Incident Reporting: P1
  - 33.3.4.1 Tools for documentation and review of:
    - 33.3.4.1.1 Adverse events
    - 33.3.4.1.2 Near-miss incidents
    - 33.3.4.1.3 Patient safety concerns
- 33.3.5 Research & Quality Improvement: P2
  - 33.3.5.1 Structured data collection features for:
    - 33.3.5.1.1 Clinical research studies
    - 33.3.5.1.2 Quality improvement programs
    - 33.3.5.1.3 Performance monitoring
- 33.3.6 Staff Overview: P1
  - 33.3.6.1 Real-time dashboard showing:
    - 33.3.6.1.1 Number of physicians, nurses, and support staff on duty
    - 33.3.6.1.2 Quick access to individual contact details and role information
- 33.3.7 Procedure Room Status: P1
  - 33.3.7.1 List of all minor treatment rooms with:
    - 33.3.7.1.1 Current occupancy status
    - 33.3.7.1.2 Maintenance status
    - 33.3.7.1.3 Expected availability
- 33.3.8 Recent Alerts/Updates: P1
  - 33.3.8.1 Real-time alerts for:
  - 33.3.8.2 Critical patient conditions
    - 33.3.8.2.1 Departmental updates and coordination notes
- 33.3.9 Active Tasks: P1 & P2
  - 33.3.9.1 Centralized to-do list for IPD teams (nurses, physicians, etc.)
  - 33.3.9.2 Task management includes: P2
    - 33.3.9.2.1 Prioritization and deadlines
    - 33.3.9.2.2 Clear assignment of responsibilities
    - 33.3.9.2.3 Status indicators (Pending, In Progress, Completed)
    - 33.3.9.2.4 Staff productivity tracking (e.g., time spent per patient)
    - 33.3.9.2.5 Dropdowns for quick task assignment (e.g., imaging, medication, blood draw)
- 33.3.10 Shift Scheduling: P1

- 33.3.10.1 Visual shift planner for efficient staff management
- 33.3.10.2 Features include:
  - 33.3.10.2.1 Real-time updates for shift changes
  - 33.3.10.2.2 Easy drag-and-drop assignment of on-call or backup staff
  - 33.3.10.2.3 Conflict detection and resolution

### **33.4 Equipment Management**

- 33.4.1 Inventory Reports: P1
  - 33.4.1.1 Generate reports on:
    - 33.4.1.1.1 Current stock levels
    - 33.4.1.1.2 Usage patterns
    - 33.4.1.1.3 Replenishment needs
  - 33.4.1.2 Track inventory turnover rates
  - 33.4.1.3 Identify equipment that is rarely used or outdated
- 33.4.2 Maintenance History Reports: P1
  - 33.4.2.1 Provide maintenance logs for each piece of equipment, including:
    - 33.4.2.1.1 Past repairs
    - 33.4.2.1.2 Servicing records
    - 33.4.2.1.3 Equipment downtime
  - 33.4.2.2 Create detailed maintenance history reports for:
    - 33.4.2.2.1 Internal reviews
    - 33.4.2.2.2 Regulatory audits
    - 33.4.2.2.3 Compliance tracking
- 33.4.3 Cost Analysis: P2
  - 33.4.3.1 Analyze costs associated with:
    - 33.4.3.1.1 Equipment maintenance
    - 33.4.3.1.2 Repairs
    - 33.4.3.1.3 Replacements
  - 33.4.3.2 Generate reports on total cost of ownership (TCO), including:
    - 33.4.3.2.1 Service contracts
    - 33.4.3.2.2 Parts and consumables
    - 33.4.3.2.3 Labor costs
- 33.4.4 Downtime Reports: P2
  - 33.4.4.1 Track and analyze equipment downtime
  - 33.4.4.2 Identify trends such as:
    - 33.4.4.2.1 Frequent breakdowns
    - 33.4.4.2.2 Long repair durations
  - 33.4.4.3 Use data to inform:
    - 33.4.4.3.1 Equipment replacement decisions
    - 33.4.4.3.2 Preventive maintenance planning

- 33.5.1.1.4 Time period

- 33.5.2.2.1 Proper stock control
- 33.5.2.2.2 Optimal usage

- 33.6.1.2 Weekly compliance summaries for hand hygiene and PPE.

- 33.6.1.4 Infection surveillance data

- 33.6.1.6 Training completion indicators for staff

33.6.3 Alerts for: P2

- 33.6.3.1 Potential outbreaks or clustering of infections.
- 33.6.3.2 Low compliance with hygiene protocols

33.6.4 Incident Reporting: P2

33.6.4.1 Digital forms for exposure or protocol breaches.

33.7.1 Blood Usage Reports: P1

33.7.1.1 Generates reports tracking blood usage, including:

- 33.7.1.1.1 Amount of blood used
- 33.7.1.1.2 Departments using the most blood
- 33.7.1.1.3 Most frequently used blood types

33.7.3.1.1 Number of donors

33.7.3.1.2 Frequency of donations

33.7.3.1.3 Blood type distribution trends

33.7.4 Compliance and Safety Audits: P1

33.7.4.1 Conducts regular audits using adverse event data, patient transfusion records, and inventory reports to ensure compliance with standards and guidelines.

33.8.1.3.3 Supports workforce scheduling management

33.8.2.1 Shift Reports: P1

33.8.2.1.1 Generate shift availability and attendance reports

33.8.2.1.2 Filters for specific dates, roles, and departments

33.8.2.2.1 Automated alerts for upcoming certification expirations

33.8.2.3.1 Track staffing gaps or excesses

33.8.2.3.2 Alerts for unfilled shift slots

33.8.3.2 Enable real-time updates for ED staff

### **33.11 Complaint Management**

33.11.1 Automated Notifications: P1

33.11.1.1 Automated alerts for newly submitted complaints

33.11.1.2 Notifications to staff when complaints are assigned or escalated

33.11.1.3 Notifications to patients when complaints are resolved or updated

33.11.4.2.1 Medical

33.11.4.2.2 Billing

33.11.4.2.3 Staff Behavior (etc.)

33.11.4.5 Follow-Up Actions:

33.11.4.5.1 Status of ongoing follow-ups

33.11.5 Complaint Resolution Dashboard: P1

33.11.5.1 Number of Resolved Complaints: [Enter number]

33.11.5.2 Number of Unresolved Complaints: [Enter number]

33.11.5.3 Escalation Rate: [Percentage of complaints escalated]

33.12.3.1 Allow ED staff to access:

33.12.3.1.1 KPIs

33.12.3.1.2 Corrective action plans

33.12.3.1.3 Clinical guidelines via mobile devices

33.12.4.1.1 Quality improvement goals

33.12.4.1.2 KPIs

## Clinical Laboratory Module

![A photograph of a modern clinical laboratory with three staff members in white lab coats working at different stations.](2d22d155e3f98dbb44dabef33853c268_img.jpg)

A photograph of a modern, well-lit clinical laboratory. Three individuals wearing white lab coats and face masks are working at different stations. In the foreground, a man is leaning over a counter, possibly performing a procedure or cleaning. In the background, two other staff members are visible at separate workstations. The laboratory is equipped with white cabinetry, red accents, and various scientific instruments. A yellow triangular warning sign is visible on the wall. The floor is white and reflective.

A photograph of a modern clinical laboratory with three staff members in white lab coats working at different stations.

## [CLINICAL LABORATORY MODULE]

### 1 Patient Management

#### 1.1 Patient Demographics Integration (P-1)

- 1.1.1 Real-time access to core patient demographics from the central Patient Registration system:
  - 1.1.1.1 Patient ID (Unique hospital-wide identifier)
  - 1.1.1.2 Name, Date of Birth, Gender
  - 1.1.1.3 Contact details (phone, email)
  - 1.1.1.4 Insurance information (if applicable)
  - 1.1.1.5 National ID/passport number
  - 1.1.1.6 Patients diagnosis/history
  - 1.1.1.7 In-case of admission patient ward, bed number
- 1.1.2 No duplicate registration; system must recognize existing patients.
- 1.1.3 New patient add only in lab (walk-in) with same above demographic details add.

#### 1.2 Visit & Encounter Information (P-1)

- 1.2.1 Link lab orders and results to specific hospital encounters:
  - 1.2.1.1 Inpatient: Admission details (ward, bed, admitting doctor)
  - 1.2.1.2 Outpatient: Clinic visit info (consulting department, doctor)
  - 1.2.1.3 Emergency: Emergency visit episode tracking
- 1.2.2 Each lab order should be traceable to a specific encounter, ensuring proper clinical

#### 1.3 Patient Status Tracking (P-1)

- 1.3.1 Live status of patient for test routing and collection:
  - 1.3.1.1 In Ward / ICU/ER
  - 1.3.1.2 At OPD
  - 1.3.1.3 Discharged (recent) – for follow-ups
  - 1.3.1.4 Home sample collection (if available)
  - 1.3.1.5 Walk-in
- 1.3.2 Helps prioritize urgent inpatient samples or coordinate home collection workflows.

#### 1.4 Lab Test Ordering Interface (P-1)

- 1.4.1 Easy, guided interface for clinicians/lab staff to order lab tests:
  - 1.4.1.1 Individual tests or predefined panels (e.g. CBC, LFT)
  - 1.4.1.2 Order priorities: Routine, Stat, Urgent
  - 1.4.1.3 Special instructions (e.g. fasting, timing)
- 1.4.2 Integration with CPOE (Computerized Physician Order Entry) or EMR module.

#### 1.5 Payment module for OPD/walk-in patients (P-1)

#### 1.6 Specimen Collection Management (P-1)

- 1.6.1 Track and manage samples linked to a patient:

- 1.6.1.1 Collection location (ward/lab/clinic/home)
- 1.6.1.2 Sample type per test
- 1.6.1.3 Time and staff who collected sample
- 1.6.1.4 Labeling with barcodes (linked to patient and visit)
- 1.6.1.5 Sample status/remarks option in-case of IPD/ER
- 1.6.2 Ensures traceability and reduces labelling errors.

## **1.7 Test History and Result Retrieval (P-1)**

- 1.7.1 Display full lab test history for each patient:
  - 1.7.1.1 Sorted by date, test type, department
  - 1.7.1.2 Comparison of past vs. current results (e.g. trend graphs) (P-2)
  - 1.7.1.3 Printable or shareable lab reports (P-1)
- 1.7.2 Integration with patient record/EMR for clinical correlation (P-1)

## **1.8 Sample Status Tracking (Per Patient) (P-1)**

- 1.8.1 Real-time status view for every sample:
  - 1.8.1.1 Ordered → Collected → Received in lab → Processing → Validated → Reported
- 1.8.2 Alerts for pending or delayed samples (pending reports)
- 1.8.3 Critical result flags with clinician notification (P-2)

## **1.9 Patient Notifications (Optional) (P-2)**

- Send patient alerts via SMS/email:
  - "Sample collected"
  - "Your lab report is ready for viewing"
  - Appointment reminder for lab test
- Requires opt-in and integration with communications module.

## **1.10 Consent & Special Handling (P-2)**

- 1.10.1 Capture patient consent for certain tests (e.g. HIV, genetic)
- 1.10.2 Notes for special patient requirements:
  - 1.10.2.1 Infectious diseases details (e.g. COVID)
  - 1.10.2.2 Allergies
  - 1.10.2.3 Language/communication needs
  - 1.10.2.4 Cultural/religious handling (e.g. for post-mortem samples)

## **1.11 Audit Trail and Data Security (P-3)**

- 1.11.1 Maintain log of all actions linked to patient:
  - 1.11.1.1 Who accessed/updated patient lab data
  - 1.11.1.2 Time-stamped modifications
- 1.11.2 Role-based access (lab techs shouldn't see unrelated patient details)

# **2 Test Management**

## **2.1 Master Test Configuration (P-1)**

- 2.1.1 Centralized **Test Master** to define all laboratory tests:

- 2.1.1.1 Test Code (unique)/CPT
- 2.1.1.2 Test Name
- 2.1.1.3 Department (e.g., Biochemistry, Microbiology, Hematology)
- 2.1.1.4 Specimen Type (e.g., Blood, Urine, Sputum, Tissue)
- 2.1.1.5 Specimen volume (in case of volume based tests e.g. CBC, LFTs etc.)
- 2.1.1.6 Container Type
- 2.1.1.7 Methodology (e.g., ELISA, PCR, Immunoassay)
- 2.1.1.8 Normal/reference ranges (age/gender-specific)
- 2.1.1.9 Critical value ranges
- 2.1.1.10 Test turnaround time (TAT)
- 2.1.1.11 Temperature requirements for sample transportation
- 2.1.2 All tests must be uniquely defined to avoid duplication and ensure consistent processing.

## **2.2 Test Categorization and Grouping**

- 2.2.1 Create Panels or Profiles: (P-1)
  - 2.2.1.1 Predefined groupings (e.g., LFT = SGOT, SGPT, Bilirubin)
  - 2.2.1.2 Customizable by hospital/lab admins
- 2.2.2 Test Types: (P-1)
  - 2.2.2.1 Routine tests
  - 2.2.2.2 Stat/Urgent tests
  - 2.2.2.3 Culture-based tests
  - 2.2.2.4 Outsourced/reference lab tests (P-2)
- 2.2.3 Panels help speed up ordering and reduce input errors.

## **2.3 Test Ordering and Management (P-1)**

- 2.3.1 Order by clinicians, nurses, or lab staff
- 2.3.2 Select test(s) for one or more patients at once
- 2.3.3 Priority tagging (Routine, Urgent, STAT)
- 2.3.4 Auto-suggest based on diagnosis/clinical pathway
- 2.3.5 Integrated with CPOE or clinician dashboard

## **2.4 Sample Requirements & Labeling (P-2)**

- 2.4.1 Auto-assign sample requirements per test
- 2.4.2 Print **barcoded labels** with patient ID, test, and specimen info
- 2.4.3 Link multiple tests to the same sample if appropriate
- 2.4.4 Prevents sample duplication and aids in chain of custody.

## **2.5 Test Routing & Workflow Management (P-1)**

- 2.5.1 Route tests to appropriate lab sections:
  - 2.5.1.1 Hematology, Clinical Chemistry, Microbiology, Pathology
- 2.5.2 Status tracking per test:
  - 2.5.2.1 Ordered → Collected → In Processing → Verified → Reported
- 2.5.3 Internal routing for multi-step workflows (e.g., culture + sensitivity)

## **2.6 Result Entry and Management**

- 2.6.1 Manual result entry interface: (P-1)

- 2.6.1.1 Numeric, text, or formatted (e.g., differential counts)
- 2.6.2 Auto-import from lab instruments (LIS interface) (P-2)
- 2.6.3 Auto-validation for non-critical normal results (optional) (P-2)
- 2.6.4 Color-coding or flagging for: (P-2)
  - 2.6.4.1 Abnormal
  - 2.6.4.2 Critical
  - 2.6.4.3 Repeat
- 2.6.5 Supports bulk entry for high-volume tests. (P-2)

## **2.7 Test Result Validation**

- 2.7.1 Multi-level validation: (P-1)
  - 2.7.1.1 Lab Technician (entry) (P-1)
  - 2.7.1.2 Pathologist/Microbiologist (validation) (P-1)
  - 2.7.1.3 Senior Consultant (final sign-off, if needed) (P-1)
- 2.7.2 Ability to return for re-testing or corrections (P-1)
- 2.7.3 Each stage is logged in the audit trail. (P-2)

## **2.8 Reference Range & Interpretation Support (P-1)**

- 2.8.1 Dynamic reference range support based on: (P-1)
  - 2.8.1.1 Age group
  - 2.8.1.2 Gender
  - 2.8.1.3 Units of measure
  - 2.8.1.4 Area/regional reference ranges
- 2.8.2 Automated interpretation text (optional) (P-2)
- 2.8.3 Trend analysis of previous test results(P-2)

## **2.9 Turnaround Time (TAT) Monitoring (P-1)**

- 2.9.1 Real-time TAT tracking by test or department
- 2.9.2 Alerts for delays or overdue processing
- 2.9.3 TAT benchmarking dashboard (configurable) (P-2)

## **2.10 Test Rejection and Re-ordering**

- 2.10.1 Reject test/sample with reasons:
  - 2.10.1.1 Hemolysis, clotted sample, insufficient volume, labeling error, temperature issue
- 2.10.2 Auto-notify ordering physician(P-2)
- 2.10.3 Workflow for re-ordering or re-collection(P-2)

## **2.11 Reporting Configuration (P-2)**

- 2.11.1 Define custom report templates per test or department
- 2.11.2 Report format support:
  - 2.11.2.1 Numeric with unit
  - 2.11.2.2 Graphical trends
  - 2.11.2.3 Image attachment (e.g. pathology slides)
- 2.11.3 Digital signature and authentication

### 3 CPT Consultant / Doctors Order

#### 3.1 Consultant Test Order Creation

##### 3.1.1 **Order Initiation:** (P-1)

Consultants should have the ability to initiate lab orders based on patient evaluations. This could be done in two main ways:

3.1.1.1 **Manual Order Entry:** The consultant manually selects the required tests from a predefined list (e.g., blood tests, microbiology, and imaging).

3.1.1.2 **Order from a Predefined Test Panel:** Consultants can select test panels (e.g., basic metabolic panel, complete blood count), which automatically populate individual test components.

##### 3.1.2 **CPT Code Integration:** (P-2)

The system must automatically associate the relevant **CPT (Current Procedural Terminology)** codes with each test or panel ordered. Each test should have an associated **CPT code** for:

3.1.2.1 **Billing:** Ensures that test orders are appropriately linked with billing codes.

3.1.2.2 **Insurance Reimbursement:** Assists with insurance claims and reimbursements.

3.1.3 Ensures accurate billing and insurance processing.

#### 3.2 Consultant-Specific Permissions & Role-based Access (P-2)

##### 3.2.1 **Role-based Access Control (RBAC):**

3.2.1.1 Consultants should have permissions to **view and order lab tests** based on their **specialization** (e.g., cardiology consultant orders cardiac biomarkers).

3.2.1.2 Lab technicians and pathologists should not be able to place consultant orders but should be able to **access and process** orders assigned to them.

3.2.1.3 The consultant's role should define access to:

3.2.1.3.1 Order status (pending, completed)

3.2.1.3.2 Patient medical history (relevant to the test)

3.2.1.3.3 Test results (after validation)

3.2.2 Ensures that only authorized users can place consultant orders and access results.

#### 3.3 Test Order Customization and Flexibility (P-2)

##### 3.3.1 **Consultant Order Preferences:**

Consultants may have personalized order sets based on their specialty. The system should allow them to:

3.3.1.1 **Save frequently ordered tests** as custom sets or panels (e.g., cardiology panel, renal panel).

3.3.1.2 Use **filtering criteria** to quickly find tests (e.g., by category, urgency, or specialty).

##### 3.3.2 **Order Details:**

For each order, the system should capture the following details:

3.3.2.1 **Patient information** (name, ID, department, bed number)

3.3.2.2 **Test name(s)** with descriptions

3.3.2.3 **CPT code(s)**

3.3.2.4 **Clinical indication** (the reason for ordering the test)

3.3.2.5 **Priority** (routine, urgent, STAT)

3.3.2.6 **Date & Time of order** (automatically captured)

3.3.3 Ensures the accuracy and relevance of ordered tests.

### 3.4 Electronic Signature and Authentication(P-2)

3.4.1 **Order Authentication:**

Once the order is created, the consultant's signature (or electronic authorization) is required to validate the order. The system should support:

3.4.1.1 Digital signature or PIN-based authentication for consultants.

3.4.1.2 Option for secondary verification (e.g., approval by a senior consultant or lab supervisor) for high-risk or high-cost tests.

3.4.2 **Audit Trails:**

Every order should be **time-stamped** with the consultant's ID and the time of the order placement, creating an **audit trail** for compliance.

3.4.3 Enhances security and ensures order validity.

### 3.5 Integration with Clinical Decision Support (CDS) (P-3)

3.5.1 **Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS):**

The system should integrate with the **CDSS** to alert consultants about:

3.5.1.1 **Test appropriateness** based on the patient's clinical profile.

3.5.1.2 **Redundant tests** that have already been ordered or performed.

3.5.1.3 **Flagging of critical or urgent tests** (e.g., for life-threatening conditions).

3.5.2 Improves test appropriateness and helps in reducing unnecessary tests.

### 3.6 Order Communication and Tracking(P-2)

3.6.1 **Order Status Tracking:**

Consultants should be able to track the status of their orders at any time, from **ordering to completion**. The system should provide:

3.6.1.1 **Real-time status updates:** Pending, in progress, completed, rejected, or cancelled.

3.6.1.2 Notifications of **test completion and availability of results**.

3.6.1.3 **Integration with the laboratory workflow** to ensure that test results are automatically linked back to the consultant's order.

3.6.2 Ensures consultants stay updated about the status and results of the tests they order.

### 3.7 Order Modification and Cancellation(P-3)

3.7.1 **Modification/Amendment:**

Consultants should have the ability to modify orders before the sample collection occurs, including:

3.7.1.1 **Changing test panels** or adding/removing specific tests.

3.7.1.2 **Adjusting the priority** (routine → urgent → STAT).

3.7.1.3 **Providing additional clinical notes** or comments.

3.7.2 **Order Cancellation:**

If needed, consultants should be able to cancel the test orders, with the cancellation properly logged in the system.

3.7.3 Allows for flexible order management and minimizes errors.

### 3.8 Test Result Delivery and Feedback

3.8.1 **Result Delivery:**

Once tests are completed and validated by lab technicians or pathologists, the results should be linked back to the consultant's original order. Consultants should:

3.8.1.1 **Receive notifications** when test results are ready. (P-3)

3.8.1.2 **Access results directly** through the EMR or lab interface. (P-1)

3.8.1.3 Option to **request additional tests** or provide feedback based on the initial results. (P-2)

3.8.2 **Result Interpretation & Clinical Notes:** (P-2)

Consultants should have the ability to:

3.8.2.1 Add **comments or clinical interpretations** on the results, if necessary.

3.8.2.2 **Order follow-up tests** or suggest **treatment modifications** based on initial findings.

3.8.3 Facilitates timely decision-making and enhances patient care.

### 3.9 Order Reporting and Analytics (P-3)

3.9.1 **Order Reports:**

The system should generate **reports on ordered tests** by consultants, including:

3.9.1.1 **Order volume analysis** by consultant, specialty, or department.

3.9.1.2 **Test utilization reports** to track the most ordered tests and identify trends.

3.9.1.3 **Time-to-result analytics**, especially for urgent or STAT orders.

3.9.2 **Consultant Performance Metrics:**

Generate reports based on consultant ordering behaviors, such as:

3.9.2.1 Average test turnaround times for each consultant.

3.9.2.2 Test panel utilization and frequency.

3.9.3 Supports operational efficiency and enhances the decision-making process.

### 3.10 Integration with Billing and Insurance Systems(P-2)

3.10.1 **Billing Integration:**

Ensure that each consultant's orders are properly linked to the hospital's billing system via the corresponding **CPT codes** for accurate billing and reimbursement.

3.10.2 **Insurance Claiming:**

The system should enable easy generation of claims for insurance processing, linking the ordered tests with **insurance codes** and **claim details**.

3.10.3 Ensures seamless billing and insurance processes.

### 3.11 Role-based Notifications(P-3)

### 3.11.1 **Notification Management:**

Consultants should receive real-time notifications regarding:

3.11.1.1 Order status changes (test ordered, test in progress, results available).

3.11.1.2 Alerts for **critical results** or **urgent requests**.

### 3.11.2 **Customized Notification Settings:**

Consultants should be able to configure their **notification preferences**, such as:

3.11.2.1 **Immediate notifications** for urgent or STAT orders.

3.11.2.2 **Scheduled notifications** (e.g., a daily report on all pending orders).

### 3.11.3 Keeps consultants informed and engaged throughout the process.

## 4 CPT Counter Order

### 4.1 **Order Creation by Non-Consultant Providers(P-3)**

#### 4.1.1 **Order Entry by Staff:**

The system should allow authorized non-consultant healthcare providers (e.g., nurses, lab technicians) to place **CPT Counter Orders** for certain tests. These tests could include:

4.1.1.1 **Routine or screening tests** (e.g., complete blood count (CBC), urinalysis).

4.1.1.2 **Follow-up lab tests** (e.g., electrolyte tests, liver function tests).

4.1.1.3 **Pre-approved panels** that do not require specific consultant review.

#### 4.1.2 **CPT Code Integration:**

Similar to consultant orders, each **CPT Counter Order** should be associated with the correct **CPT code** for billing and insurance purposes, ensuring accurate billing and reimbursement. This code should be automatically assigned when the test is selected.

#### 4.1.3 Ensures accuracy in billing and efficient insurance claim processing.

### 4.2 **Role-based Permissions & Access Control(P-3)**

#### 4.2.1 **Authorized User Access: (P-3)**

The system must define who can place CPT Counter Orders, typically allowing:

4.2.1.1 **Nurses** to place orders for routine tests (e.g., routine blood work).

4.2.1.2 **Lab Technicians** to place orders for follow-up tests.

4.2.1.3 **Phlebotomists** for certain pre-approval tests based on patient care pathways.

#### 4.2.2 **Role-Specific Restrictions: (P-3)**

4.2.2.1 The system should **restrict certain tests** from being ordered by non-consultant providers (e.g., high-complexity tests or tests that require a consultant's clinical interpretation).

4.2.2.2 Only authorized users (e.g., nurses, lab technicians) can access the **CPT Counter Order functionality**, and their access should be limited based on their specific role and responsibility.

#### 4.2.3 Ensures that only authorized personnel can place orders and minimizes the risk of unauthorized or inappropriate test requests.

### 4.3 **Predefined Test Sets or Panels for Counter Orders(P-2)**

#### 4.3.1 **Preconfigured Order Sets:**

The system should allow for predefined test sets or panels for commonly ordered tests. These might include:

4.3.1.1 **Routine test panels:** e.g., CBC, lipid profile, basic metabolic panel (BMP).

4.3.1.2 **Screening tests:** e.g., diabetes screening (HbA1c), cholesterol levels.

4.3.1.3 **Follow-up tests:** e.g., liver enzyme tests for patients undergoing treatment.

#### 4.3.2 **Customizable Test Lists:**

Healthcare providers should be able to access a **customized list of available tests** based on their role or specialty area (e.g., nurses may have access to a list of pre-approved screening tests, while lab technicians may access follow-up test options).

#### 4.3.3 **Test Panel Grouping:**

The system should automatically group tests that are often ordered together (e.g., liver function tests, kidney function tests) to make it easier for non-consultant staff to place comprehensive orders.

4.3.4 Streamlines ordering process and ensures common tests are easily accessible.

### 4.4 **Order Details and Information Capture(P-1)**

For each **CPT Counter Order**, the system should capture the following details:

4.4.1 **Patient Information** (name, ID, department, ward, clinician).

4.4.2 **Test(s) Ordered:** With the ability to specify a list of tests or a predefined panel.

4.4.3 **Priority Level** (e.g., routine, STAT, urgent).

4.4.4 **Order Date/Time:** Automatically populated by the system when the order is placed.

4.4.5 **Clinical Notes/Justification** (optional, if required): The healthcare provider may enter brief comments or reasons for the test, especially if there are specific patient conditions or follow-up needs.

4.4.6 **CPT Code:** The system should automatically generate the appropriate CPT code based on the test or panel ordered.

4.4.7 Ensures all necessary information is captured for both clinical and billing purposes.

### 4.5 **Authorization and Approval Workflow(P-3)**

#### 4.5.1 **Verification Process:**

Although **CPT Counter Orders** are initiated by non-consultant staff, the system should ensure that these orders are **reviewed and verified** by a senior staff member before they are processed, especially for tests that have implications on patient care or billing. The process can include:

4.5.1.1 **Supervisor review:** Senior nurses, clinical leads, or laboratory managers may be required to review orders before they are sent to the lab.

4.5.1.2 **Consultant validation:** In certain cases, the system may automatically prompt a **consultant for confirmation** before processing the order.

4.5.2 **Audit Trail & Tracking: (P-3)**

All **CPT Counter Orders** should be logged in the system with complete **audit trail functionality**, capturing:

4.5.2.1 User details (who placed the order),

4.5.2.2 Date and time of the order,

4.5.2.3 Any amendments or cancellations to the order,

4.5.2.4 Status updates (e.g., pending, in progress, completed).

4.5.3 Enhances accountability and ensures compliance with hospital and regulatory standards.

#### 4.6 Test Status Tracking and Notifications(P-2)

4.6.1 **Real-Time Status Updates:**

The system should allow the person who placed the order to track the order's **status** at all times. This includes:

4.6.1.1 **Pending:** Order has been created but not yet processed by the laboratory.

4.6.1.2 **In Progress:** The test is being performed.

4.6.1.3 **Completed:** The test is complete, and results are ready.

4.6.1.4 **Rejected or Canceled:** If there's an issue with the order, such as missing information or an invalid test request.

4.6.2 **Notifications: (P-3)**

Non-consultant staff should receive **notifications** when:

4.6.2.1 The order is completed.

4.6.2.2 Test results are available for review.

4.6.2.3 Any issues with the order are detected (e.g., if a required field is missing or if the order is rejected).

4.6.3 Ensures real-time awareness and faster decision-making for healthcare providers.

#### 4.7 Order Modification and Cancellation(P-2)

4.7.1 **Order Modification:**

The system should allow non-consultant staff to **modify** the order before the test begins, including:

4.7.1.1 Changing test types (e.g., from basic metabolic panel to advanced panel),

4.7.1.2 Updating test priority (e.g., routine to STAT),

4.7.1.3 Adding or removing tests from an ordered panel.

4.7.2 **Order Cancellation:**

In the event of an error, the **CPT Counter Order** should be easily cancellable before the test is performed, with proper **audit tracking** to log who cancelled the order and why.

4.7.3 Ensures flexibility and error correction in the ordering process.

#### 4.8 Integration with Billing and Insurance (P-2)

#### 4.8.1 **CPT Code Integration:**

Each **CPT Counter Order** should be tied to an accurate **CPT code**, ensuring that the system generates the correct billing data when the test is completed. The system should automatically populate the **CPT code** when a test or panel is selected.

#### 4.8.2 **Insurance Claim Management:**

The system should provide an **interface** for generating **insurance claims** based on the CPT codes associated with the CPT Counter Order. This ensures that tests performed under **CPT Counter Orders** are accurately billed to patients or insurance companies.

#### 4.8.3 Simplifies billing and insurance claims.

### 4.9 **Reporting and Analytics(P-2)**

#### 4.9.1 **Order Analysis Reports:**

The system should provide detailed reports on **CPT Counter Orders**, including:

4.9.1.1 **Test volume trends:** Number of CPT Counter Orders over time, broken down by test type or department.

4.9.1.2 **Test request origin:** Reports identifying which non-consultant staff placed the orders, helping identify high-volume users or departments.

4.9.1.3 **Test completion times:** Time taken for tests to be processed and results delivered.

4.9.1.4 **Tests counts-statics**

#### 4.9.2 **Financial Reporting:**

Generate reports showing the total revenue generated from CPT Counter Orders based on CPT codes, including:

4.9.2.1 Test cost per order

4.9.2.2 Insurance reimbursement status

#### 4.9.3 Helps hospital management with operational and financial insights.

### 4.10 **Security & Compliance(P-3)**

#### 4.10.1 **Audit Trails:**

All CPT Counter Orders must have a secure audit trail to track:

4.10.1.1 Who placed the order?

4.10.1.2 Who reviewed or approved it.

4.10.1.3 Any modifications or cancellations made to the order.

4.10.1.4 When results were reported or notified to the relevant stakeholders.

#### 4.10.2 **Data Privacy & Compliance:**

The system must comply with relevant healthcare data privacy laws (e.g., **HIPAA** or **GDPR**), ensuring that patient data is secure and access to orders is restricted to authorized users.

#### 4.10.3 Maintains compliance and ensures security of patient and order data.

## 5 **Sample Collection & Tracking**

### 5.1 **Sample Collection Request & Authorization(P-2)**

#### 5.1.1 Automatically generate **sample collection requests** when lab tests are ordered.

- 5.1.2 Allow authorized users (nurses, phlebotomists, technicians) to view pending collections:
  - 5.1.2.1 Grouped by patient, department, or time slots
- 5.1.3 Integration with **CPOE or clinician orders** to ensure no unapproved collection occurs
- 5.1.4 All sample collection must be traceable to a valid order.

## 5.2 Sample Collection Assignment and Tracking(P-1)

- 5.2.1 **Order Assignment:**  
Phlebotomists should receive **assigned tasks** for sample collection based on orders placed by consultants or non-consultant healthcare providers. These tasks should be automatically assigned through the HMIS when a test is ordered, with the system generating:
  - 5.2.1.1 **Patient information** (name, ID, room number, etc.).
  - 5.2.1.2 **Test details** (type of sample, tests requested, priority level).
  - 5.2.1.3 **Location details** (ward, department, bed number).
- 5.2.2 **Task Visibility:**  
The phlebotomist must be able to **view all assigned sample collection tasks** in real-time, including any **urgent or STAT** requests. They should also have access to the **patient's clinical history** (e.g., allergies, previous lab results) to ensure proper sample collection.
- 5.2.3 Ensures that phlebotomists are aware of their tasks and that they can prioritize urgent samples.

## 5.3 Phlebotomist Acknowledgement of Collection Tasks(P-1)

- 5.3.1 **Acknowledgement Process:**  
The phlebotomist should be able to **acknowledge** receipt of the sample collection task in the system. This can be done through:
  - 5.3.1.1 **Click-to-confirm acknowledgement:** When the phlebotomist receives an assigned sample collection task, they should click a button to acknowledge that they have received the task and are starting the process.
